macro_segments.title: The Daughters of Iyov and the Blessing of Increase versus Strife

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish debate whether daughters bring increase or strife to the world
Why Iyov's daughters were not doubled in number but in beauty, explained through their names Yemima, Ketziah, and Keren Hapuch
Teaching of the Tannaim: the world needs both males and females, though males are praised and females lamented
Interpretation of 'God blessed Abraham with everything' (bakol): various views on sons, daughters, and Abraham's merits

macro_segments.title: The Death of the Righteous and the Preservation of Bodies

micro_segments.title: Three people who tasted the world to come: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, blessed with bakol, mikkol, kol
Three people over whom the evil inclination had no mastery: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
Six people over whom the angel of death had no mastery, dying with the divine kiss: patriarchs, Moses, Aaron, and Miriam
Seven people whose bodies were not consumed by worms or maggots, including Benjamin and David
Four people who died only on account of the serpent's deed in the Garden of Eden, without personal sin

macro_segments.title: Digging Cisterns and Wells: Distance Requirements from Neighbor's Property

micro_segments.title: A person cannot dig a cistern, ditch, cave, or irrigation canal adjacent to his neighbor's well without maintaining distance and plastering
Piles of materials that give off heat (chaff, excrement, salt, plaster, rocks) must be kept three handbreadths from neighbor's wall
Seeds, plowing, and urination must be kept three handbreadths from neighbor's wall
Millstones and ovens have specific distance requirements measured from their parts

macro_segments.title: The Wall of the Cistern as Standard Width and Its Implications

micro_segments.title: The wall of a cistern has a standard width of three handbreadths, establishing the distance requirement
A sale of a cistern and its wall requires the wall to be three handbreadths wide

macro_segments.title: Debate: Can One Dig a Cistern at the Property Boundary Line?

micro_segments.title: Abaye: In a field not normally suited for cisterns, one may dig right up to the boundary line
Rava: One cannot dig a cistern right at the boundary line even if the field is not normally used for cisterns
Explanation of the debate through the lens of responsibility: the damager (mazik) versus the damaged party (nizak)
Even Rabbi Yose, who permits planting a tree by an existing cistern, would not allow digging at the boundary because it weakens the ground

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Mishna: Why the Wall is Mentioned and Distance Always Required

micro_segments.title: According to Rava, the Mishna's mention of a wall teaches that even the first person to dig cannot go to the boundary line
The Mishna's scenario of an existing cistern with a wall is Rava's way of showing the distance requirement applies universally
Abaye's alternative: the Mishna describes both parties digging simultaneously, so both must share the distance equidistantly
